items.q: Why does the cognitive approach use INFERENCE?
items.correct: Mental processes cannot be directly observed, so they are inferred from behaviour.
items.misconception: Because cognitive psychologists guess instead of measuring.
items.why: Inference is a methodological necessity, not a weakness of rigour. Students treat it as vague guessing. It means drawing conclusions about unobservable processes from observable behaviour.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.q: When new info pushes out old, it's…
items.why: It's Interference. “Decay” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Memory trick. “Brain region” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Recommended sleep for teens, hours per night?
items.why: The real figure is 9. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
